coredata.dc:description: The development of a low-cost and high-performance electrode for water electrolysis is important for green energy development. In this paper, fluorine-based multi-halogen atom doped carbon quantum dot loaded vertical graphene electrodes are prepared using biomass waste vinasse as a carbon source. F and Cl co-doping has the best electrochemical properties among multi-halogen atom doping. The HER overpotentials of FCl-CQDs/VG at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 are 171 mV and 161 mV at 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, and the OER overpotentials are 393 mV and 556 mV at 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. The Faraday efficiencies of FCl-CQDs/VG are 89 % (HER) and 86.5 % (OER), respectively. Cl atom doping can increase the content of ionic and semi-ionic bonds C–F, accelerate charge transfer, and improve electrochemical performance. This study improves the basis and ideas for the development of low-cost and high-performance self-supported all-carbon electrodes.

XPS survey pattern (a) C 1s, (b) F 1s, (c) Cl 2p regions of FCl-CQDs/VG and (d) F 1s region of F-CQDs/VG; (e) Cl 2p regions of Cl-CQDs/VG.

LSV curves of Pt/C-CQDs/VG, FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG for HER in 1 M KOH (a) and 0.5 M H2SO4 (c), and their Tafel slopes in 1 M KOH (b) and 0.5 M H2SO4 (d). Free energy diagrams of HER on FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG (a); Schematic representation of C–F bond changes after Cl atom doping of F-CQDs/VG (b); Schematic representation of the effect of halogen atom doping on electrochemical activity.

The Nyquist plots of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG (a); HER and OER chronoamperometric curves of the FCl-CQDs/VG in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 10 mA cm−2 current density (b); Cdl values of the FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH (c); ECSA values of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH (d); Overall water splitting tests of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG and FI-CQDs/VG as cathode and anode in 1.0 M KOH (e) and 0.5 M H2SO4 (f); i-t curves of FCl-CQDs/VG for cathodic and anodic water splitting tests in 1 M KOH(g) and 0.5 M H2SO4(h) solutions.

Experimental volumes of FCl-CQDs/VG (a), FBr-CQDs/VG (c), and FI-CQDs/VG (e) for the production of H2 and O2 in a sealed H-type electrolyzer with a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Faraday efficiencies of FCl-CQDs/VG(b), FBr-CQDs/VG(d), FI-CQDs/VG(f) electrodes for the production of H2 and O2 during the water separation process.
XPS full spectrum (a) and fine spectral data (b) C 1s, (c) F 1s, (d) Cl 2p, (e) Br 3d, (f) I 3d of FClBrI-CQDs/VG.
Comparison of the performance of FCl-CQDs/VG with recently reported all-carbon based catalytic electrodes for HER (a) and OER (b) in 1 M KOH and HER (c) in 0.5 M H2SO4.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a low-cost and high-performance electrode for water electrolysis is important for green energy development. In this paper, fluorine-based multi-halogen atom doped carbon quantum dot loaded vertical graphene electrodes are prepared using biomass waste vinasse as a carbon source. F and Cl co-doping has the best electrochemical properties among multi-halogen atom doping. The HER overpotentials of FCl-CQDs/VG at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 are 171 mV and 161 mV at 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, and the OER overpotentials are 393 mV and 556 mV at 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. The Faraday efficiencies of FCl-CQDs/VG are 89 % (HER) and 86.5 % (OER), respectively. Cl atom doping can increase the content of ionic and semi-ionic bonds C–F, accelerate charge transfer, and improve electrochemical performance. This study improves the basis and ideas for the development of low-cost and high-performance self-supported all-carbon electrodes.

ce:section.ce:para: The depletion of fossil energy and the deterioration of the ecological environment have made renewable energy highly valued and vigorously developed [1–3]. Water electrolysis technology has received a lot of attention for its ability to produce high purity zero-carbon green hydrogen [4–6]. However, HER and OER reactions have high energy barriers, and catalytic electrodes are needed to reduce the energy barriers [7,8]. Commercial electrocatalysts commonly used for water electrolysis include Pt/C, IrO2/RuO2. The scarcity and high cost of precious metals affect the large-scale popularization of water electrolysis technology [9,10]. Carbon-based catalysts have received widespread attention due to their low cost, high conductivity, and easy regulation [11,12]. Among many carbon-based electrocatalysts, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are candidates for fast electrochemical reactions due to their excellent biocompatibility, high chemical stability, low fabrication cost, high electrical conductivity, and surface-tunable function. CQDs loaded on vertical graphene (VG) self-supported electrodes as electron acceptors can significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency and electrocatalytic activity of graphene substrates [13,14]. Therefore, combining CQDs and VG is an effective strategy to further enhance their physicochemical properties.

On the other hand, the main raw material for the preparation of graphene is still graphite powder, which is not only detrimental to the environmental sustainability, but also contributes to the high cost of graphene. It has been demonstrated in the literature that cellulose-containing biomass materials such as rice husk and straw are widely used as carbon sources for carbon material preparation due to their wide availability, low cost, and renewability [15–17]. We have used biomass vinasse to prepare high-performance catalytic materials CQDs by hydrothermal method, and it is expected to be further prepared into VG electrodes loaded with CQDs [13]. Halogenation is an effective strategy for the modulation of the morphology and chemical properties of graphene-based electrodes, not only to increase the defects on the electrode surface, but also to form strongly reactive C-halogen bonds [18–20]. F, as a representative element of the halogen group of atoms, has the strongest electronegativity in the periodic table. When F is doped into graphene-based electrodes, it can maximize the charge polarization of adjacent C atoms and improve the electrochemical activity of the electrode [21–24]. Other atoms in the halogen group of elements Cl, Br, and I have also been progressively developed for their doping roles. Chlorination can even be more effective than fluorination in lowering energy levels and improving molecular stacking [25]. Br doping can induce the formation of oxygen vacancies on the surface of the catalytic electrode [26]. I doping inhibits the formation of less reactive graphite N and induces the production of pyridine and pyrrole N [27]. Some scholars have investigated the effect of co-doping of two different halogen elements on the electrochemical performance of the electrode, and found that the interaction of two different halogen elements such as (F, Cl co-doping and F, I co-doping) can further promote the performance of the electrode in the water decomposition and supercapacitor [28,29]. However, there is no systematic study on the catalytic electrodes co-doped with multiple halogen atoms based on F doping.

In this paper, we prepare CQDs and VG by hydrothermal method and cellulose alkalization treatment using industrial waste vinasse as a carbon source.We construct F-based multi-halogen atoms doped CQDs loaded VG electrode as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting (OWS). The optimal doping form of F-based multi-halogen atoms and the synergistic effect of the halogen atoms with the CQDs/VG electrodes are systematically elucidated by electrochemical tests, physical characterization and DFT calculations. The low-cost, high-performance all-carbon catalytic electrode lowered the energy barrier of hydrogen production from electrolyzed water, met the requirements of recycling and clean production, and was of great significance in alleviating energy problems.

The appropriate amount of vinegar residue was carbonized in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 6 h. The carbonized vinegar residue and distilled water were taken and added to a PTFE reactor at a mass of 1:10, and then hydrothermally heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. The supernatant was centrifuged and filtered to obtain CQDs.

The vinegar residue was mixed with 1 % mass fraction sulfuric acid in the ratio of 1 g:20 mL in an oil bath at 120 °C for 1 h, washed and dried with water at the end of the oil bath. The product obtained in the ratio of 1 g: 30 mL with 5 % mass fraction sodium hydroxide solution oil bath 120 °C 1 h, after the end of the water washing and drying to obtain cellulose. Cellulose and FeCl3 aqueous solution were stirred and mixed in the ratio of 6 g:50 mL for 2 h, and dried in an oven at 120 °C to obtain precursor 1. Precursor 1 was placed into a tube furnace and calcined at 350 °C for 2–3 h in N2 atmosphere, and then heated up to 1050 °C to complete the 3 h graphitization calcination. The obtained product was acid washed 4 times with hydrochloric acid and 4 times with distilled water, then dried and oxidized with sulfuric acid, KMnO4 and H2O2. Finally, it was washed again with hydrochloric acid and distilled water and dried to obtain vinasse-based graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) catalytic electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs prepared above were added to the centrifuge tube 1:1 with distilled water, and then the prepared VG tablets were mixed with the CQDs solution in the centrifuge tube at a mass of 0.12 g:10 ml. The solution was further sonicated and pulverized for 2 h. Different concentrations of dopant NaF (10, 30, 50, 70 mg/10 ml) were added during sonication. Finally, 300 μl of anhydrous ethanol was added and the resulting solution was freeze-dried on a copper disk of 4 cm diameter for 24 h. Different concentrations of X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) catalytic electrodes precursors could be obtained. The obtained precursors were calcined at 800 °C for 3 h in a tube furnace with N2 atmosphere to obtain X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) catalytic electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FX-CQDs/VG (combination of X = Cl, Br, I) catalytic electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In the preparation of F-CQDs/VG 30 mg NaF was changed to 15 mg NaF and 15 mg NaCl, followed by freeze-drying and high-temperature calcination, and the sample obtained was FCl-CQDs/VG. FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG, FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, FClBrI-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes were prepared according to the above scheme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pt/C-CQDs/VG and IrO2-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Add 5 mg of 20 % Pt/C or IrO2 catalyst to a 2 mL centrifuge tube, followed by 90 μL of deionized water, 900 μL of isopropanol, and 10 μL of nafion perfluororesin solution. After ultrasonically mixing well, the surface of CQDs/VG was uniformly drop-coated until saturated, and Pt/C-CQDs/VG and IrO2-CQDs/VG were obtained by stationary drying for 10–15 min.

Scanning electronic microscope (SEM) images were gained by using a Zeiss Gemini 500 field emission SEM (Zeiss Co., Germany). The X–ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of synthesized materials were obtained with a Bruker–AxsD8 X–ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV and 50 mA (Bruker Axs Co., Germany). X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI-5000 VPIII) was acquired on an X–ray photoelectron spectrometer system (ESCALAB Xi+).

The electrochemical performance of the catalytic electrodes was tested by the electrochemical workstation CHI660E using a three-electrode system. The electrochemical performance of the catalyst was tested in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytes, with the catalyst as the working electrode, graphite rods as counter electrodes, and Hg/HgO and Hg/HgSO4 as reference electrodes. LSV was used to test at the scanning rate of 5 mV s−1, and 95 % infrared compensation was carried out in the test. Before EIS measurement the open circuit voltage was tested, and applied in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. CV was tested at sweep speeds of 5, 10, 25, 50, 100 mV s−1. The stabilities of the catalytic electrodes were tested for 10 h by CP method. All tests were conducted at room temperature. All measured potentials of the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) were calculated using the following equation:

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) is calculated from the normalized Cdl with the following equation:

All the Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed by the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP5.4.4) [30]. The electron exchange correlation function was described using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof function(PBE), and the interactions between the nuclei and electrons of atoms were defined using the projector augmented wave(PAW) [31,32]. The electron's wave function expanded on a plane wave basis with a cutoff energy of 500 eV. The convergence units of energy and force in the process of structural relaxation were respectively set as 10−5 and 10−4. During the calculations, a 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-points grid was used. To avoid the effects of periodic structures, all computational models added the 15 Å vacuum layer horizontally.

SEM images of the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes were shown in Fig. 1 a and b. It could be seen that the graphene sheets were laminated to form a three-dimensional vertical array structure (VG) (a) and an interconnected porous network (b). This unique 3D VG structure enlarged the specific surface area of the catalytic electrode, increased the contact between the catalyst and the electrolyte, and also promoted the gas release. The formation of three-dimensional vertical array structures was attributed to the effect of directional freezing after sonication. Therefore, we only showed the morphology of the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. Fig. 1c showed the EDS elemental mapping pattern of the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. From the mapping diagram, it could be seen that the elements C, F, and Cl were uniformly distributed on the surface of the catalyst, indicating that the halogenated elements were successfully doped during the preparation of the catalytic electrode. The elemental mapping diagrams of other catalytic electrodes were displayed in Fig. S1. This indicated the successful preparation of halogen atom doped vinasse based catalytic electrode. CQDs had a strong absorption of UV light (Fig. 1d). The absorption band around 270 nm was attributed to the π-π* leap of the C═C bond. The broad peak between 300 nm and 400 nm was attributed to the n-π* leap of the C═O bond, which indicated the successful generation of CQD [33]. The successful doping of CQDs with a particle size of about 10 nm approximately could also be seen in the TEM image of FCl-CQDs/VG in Fig. 1e. Fig. 1f Raman spectra of CQDs/VG doped with different halogen atoms indicated to some extent the role of halogen atom doping on the formation of substrate defects. The D peak at 1320 cm−1 and the G peak at 1580 cm−1 corresponded to the disordered vibration and planar vibration of carbon atoms, respectively. The intensity ratios of the D peaks to the G peaks (ID: IG) could reflect the disorderedness of the material and the degree of defects [34]. It showed that the catalytic electrodes brought more disorder and surface defects when the Cl element was further doped into the F-CQDs/VG, which could be attributed to the synergistic effect of F and Cl. However, when Br and I atoms were further doped led to a decrease in the degree of defects, which could be attributed to the excessively large atomic size that would lead to spatial site resistance. On the other hand, Br and I atoms might occupy the defect sites between 2D and 3D materials [35].

The XPS measurement spectra of FCl-CQDs/VG confirmed the presence of halogen elements F, Cl as shown in Fig. S2. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of FCl-CQDs/VG with fitted peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, and 286.5 eV corresponded to CC/C–C, C–O–H, and C–F/C–Cl, respectively (Fig. 2 a). This indicated the successful doping of F and Cl atoms on CQDs/VG. The high-resolution XPS map of Cl 2p in Fig. 2c revealed the existence of two different types of C–Cl bonds in FCl-CQDs/VG, ion-bonded chlorine and covalent C–Cl bonds, respectively [36,37]. The fitted peaks at lower binding energies of 198.59 eV and 200.12 eV were corresponded to ionically bonded chlorine Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, and the ionic Cl could act as an electron acceptor to increase the charge transfer rate. The fitted peaks at higher binding energies 201.6eV and 203.2eV were corresponded to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 orbitals of covalently bonded C–Cl. Fig. 2e represented the Cl 2p high-resolution XPS map of Cl-CQDs/VG. The fitted peaks at binding energies 198.5 eV and 200.1 eV correspond to ionically bonded chlorine, and the fitted peaks at binding energies 199.3 eV and 201 correspond to covalently bonded C–Cl. The ionically bonded chlorine content of Cl-CQDs/VG was about 69 %, whereas the ionically bonded chlorine of FCl-CQDs/VG was only 57 %, which was a decrease of 12 %. This might be due to the fact that after doping of Cl atoms into F-CQDs/VG, F had greater electronegativity compared to Cl, which would produce a stronger attraction for electrons and a decrease in electrons for ion-bonded chlorine, which also corresponded to an increase in the spectral binding energy of Cl 2p. Fig. 2b and d represented the high-resolution F 1s spectra of FCl-CQDs/VG and F-CQDs/VG, respectively. The fitted peaks at 684.13 eV, 686.05 eV, and 688.2 eV in the F 1s spectra in Fig. 2b correspond to ionic, semi-ionic, and covalent bonded C–F, respectively [38]. The contents of ionic, semi-ionic, and covalent bonds C–F were about 44.5 %, 38.6 %, and 16.9 %. Ionic bonds C–F had the properties of a conductor and could efficiently transport charge. High covalent bond C–F ratios might disrupt the conductive network of the conjugated double bond and thus exhibit insulating properties. Semi-ionic bonds C–F were intermediate between ionic and covalent bonds, which had the greatest polarity and played a major role in electrochemical processes [39,40]. The fitted peaks of the F 1s spectra at 684.8 eV, 686.6 eV, and 688.4 eV in Fig. 2d correspond to ionically bonded, semi-ionically bonded, and covalently bonded C–F. The contents of ionic, semi-ionic, and covalent bonds C–F were about 43.8 %, 14.9 %, and 41.2 %. Further doping of F-CQDs/VG with Cl atoms could effectively increase the content of ionic and semi-ionic C–F bonds, and the formation of a large number of semi-ionic C–F bonds greatly promotes the charge transfer rate of the catalytic electrode. After doping of Cl atoms, the bonding of F 1s was negatively shifted, which implied that the adsorption of F atoms to the surrounding electrons was further enhanced, and H+ was more readily adsorbed to produce H2.

We designed the catalytic electrode X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) with different dopant concentrations (10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg, 40 mg, 50 mg, 60 mg, 70 mg) for LSV tests in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions in Figs. S3, S4, S5 and S6. From Figs. S3, S4, S5 and S6, it could be seen that the halogenated elements appeared to have the best dopant concentration within the dopant range, and the catalytic electrode of 30 mg showed superior electrochemical performance among all dopant concentration catalytic electrodes. This indicated that the halogenated elements showed the best performance within a certain concentration interval, which was similar to the findings of many scholars that the halide concentration changed the electronic structure of the catalysts to varying degrees, and the electronic structure of the catalytic electrodes changed favorably only within a specific range of halide concentrations [22,41]. Considering the effect of multi-doped dopant on the performance of the catalytic electrode, the optimal doping content was determined by single doping, and the dopant content was based on the optimal doping content in the subsequent experiments.

The HER overpotentials of F-CQDs/VG, Cl-CQDs/VG, Br-CQDs/VG, and I-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes were 220 mV, 240 mV, 297 mV, and 329 mV, respectively, when the current density was up to 10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH solution (Fig. S7a). Tafel slopes were calculated from the LSV data, and the corresponding Tafel slopes for the above electrocatalysts were 83 mV dec−1, 106 mV dec−1, 138 mV dec−1, and 162 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. S7b). In 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, the HER overpotentials were 290 mV, 311 mV, 335 mV, and 390 mV for F-CQDs/VG, Cl-CQDs/VG, Br-CQDs/VG, and I-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes, respectively, when the current density reached 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. S7c). The Tafel slopes were 110 mV dec−1, 121 mV dec−1, 132 mV dec−1, and 147 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. S7d). Among the single halogen atom doped electrodes, the F-CQDs/VG electrode had the lowest Tafel value with the fastest HER reaction rate. Figs. S7e and g represented the LSV curves of X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) in 1 M KOH solution and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. The OER overpotentials of F-CQDs/VG, Cl-CQDs/VG, Br-CQDs/VG and I-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes in 1 M KOH solution were 451 mV, 489 mV, 542 mV and 616 mV, corresponding to Tafel slopes of 221 mV dec−1, 263 mV dec−1, 333 mV dec−1 and 341 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. S7f). The OER overpotentials in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were 500 mV, 573 mV, 601 mV, and 642 mV (Fig. S7g), corresponding to Tafel slopes of 201 mV dec−1, 221 mV dec−1, 312 mV dec−1, and 343 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. S7h). From the above results, it could be concluded that halogen doping could further improve the catalytic activity of CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes, in which the effect of F doping was the most significant. It was shown that among the electrochemical properties of halogen-doped graphene, I-doped graphene had the slowest rate of heterogeneous electron transfer, while F-doped graphene had the fastest rate of heterogeneous electron transfer, which was consistent with the trend of electronegativity (3.98 for F, 3.16 for Cl, 2.96 for Br, and 2.66 for I). The high electronegativity of the fluorine atom produced a strong electron absorption effect. The water molecules around the electrode more easily obtained electrons, accelerating electron adsorption and gas generation [42].

Figs. S8a and b represented the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I). The Cdl values of F-CQDs/VG, Cl-CQDs/VG, Br-CQDs/VG, and I-CQDs/VG were 9 mF cm−2, 7.2 mF cm−2, 5.9 mF cm−2, and 4 mF cm−2. ECSA measured the size of the area of the catalyst involved in the reaction, and the ECSA value was positively correlated with the Cdl value. The F-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode had the largest ECSA value, which was in agreement with the LSV results. The EIS values of F-CQDs/VG, Cl-CQDs/VG, Br-CQDs/VG, and I-CQDs/VG were 10 Ω, 13 Ω, 16 Ω, and 19 Ω, respectively, as seen in Fig. S8c and F-CQDs/VG has the smallest EIS value in X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I). The semi-ionic bond C–F promotes charged transfer and electron transport, which contributed to the migration of charge carriers, and had the smallest charge-transfer resistance [42,43]. The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of X-CQDs/VG (X = F, Cl, Br, I) showed that the diffraction peak at 26.2° corresponded to the (002) face of graphitic carbon (PDF#41–1487) (Fig. S8d). As the size of the halogen atoms increases, the position of the diffraction peak at 26.2° gradually decreased and broadened, and the halogen atoms were further successfully doped into the graphene substrate as shown by the XRD results [44].

The synergistic effect of multiple heteroatoms might further enhance the catalytic activity of the catalytic electrodes [45]. We further introduced other halogen atoms on the basis of F-doped CQDs/VG to study the effect on the catalytic electrodes. In order to obtain more realistic catalytic electrode potentials, the EIS test results of the catalytic electrodes were fitted using Zview software to obtain accurate solution resistance Rs, and then the LSV curves were IR compensated. The corresponding IR compensation curves were shown in Fig. S9. Fig. 3 a showed the LSV curves of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and Pt/C- CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH. Among the same type of catalytic electrodes, FCl-CQDs/VG had the lowest overpotential. The overpotential was 171 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which was closest to 95 mV for the noble metal catalytic electrode Pt/C–C-CQDs/VG. The overpotentials of FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG were 247 mV, 269 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. From Fig. 3b, it could be seen that the tafel slopes of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and Pt/C- CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH were 87 mV dec−1, 115 mV dec−1, 141 mV dec−1 and 46 mV dec−1, respectively, which were consistent with the patterns of LSV curves. Fig. 3c represented the LSV curves of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and Pt/C- CQDs/VG in 0.5 M H2SO4. Among the same type of catalytic electrodes, FCl-CQDs/VG had the lowest overpotential, which was 161 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. It was only 39 mV lower than that of the noble metal catalytic electrode Pt/C-CQDs/VG. The overpotentials of FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG were 184 mV and 209 mV. In Fig. 3d it could be seen that in 0.5 M H2SO4 FCl-CQDs/VG still had a minimum tafel slope of 77 mV dec−1. According to the tafel value of HER of FCl-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, the decisive rate step was the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism (H2O + M + e−→M-Hads + OH− and M-Hads + H2O + e−→M + H2+OH−). The high electronegativity of F and Cl atoms with respect to C atoms led to the attraction of electrons from the surrounding C atoms to F, Cl atoms. The high electron density of F, Cl atoms adsorbed H* and H2O* for the HER reaction, and the F, Cl sites were the active sites for the HER reaction. The water electrolysis process was categorized into HER and OER processes, and we also tested the OER performance of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG under alkaline and acidic environments. Fig. 3e and g showed the LSV curves of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and IrO2-CQDs/VG at 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. It could be seen that the trends of OER catalytic activity and HER catalytic activity of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and IrO2-CQDs/VG were similar. FCl-CQDs/VG had the smallest OER overpotentials among the same type of catalytic electrodes in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, which were 393 mV and 556 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. It was smaller than 472 mV and 582 mV for FBr-CQDs/VG and 483 mV and 622 mV for FI-CQDs/VG, and closest to 270 mV and 264 mV for IrO2-CQDs/VG. At a current density of 10 mA cm−2, the tafel slopes of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG and IrO2-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH were 296 mV dec−1, 335 mV dec−1, 347 mV dec−1, 140 mV dec−1 (Fig. 3f), and in 0.5 M H2SO4 were 321 mV dec−1, 344 mV dec−1, 417 mV dec−1, 227 mV dec−1(Fig. 3h). This was in agreement with the trend obtained from the LSV curves. The minimum tafel value of FCl-CQDs/VG indicated that it had the fastest catalytic kinetics and the best catalytic activity among the same type of catalytic electrodes. Fluorine had the strongest electronegativity in the periodic table of the elements, and it induced a strong charge redistribution by adsorbing electrons around it. Many inter- or intramolecular non-covalent interactions were formed, including the three types of C–F bonds, covalent, semi-ionic and ionic, favoring the planarity of the molecule [46]. F atom adsorbed electrons around the C atom, increasing the electron density around the F site and forming a positively charged C atom. The utilization of a large number of C sites as active sites for intermediate adsorption facilitated the adsorption of intermediates OH*, O*, and OOH*. Chlorine had empty 3d orbitals that could accept electron pairs/π electrons, and although the electronegativity of chlorine was less than that of fluorine, chlorine had a greater electron affinity compared to fluorine, which could further change the electronic structure [47,48]. Cl doping coupled with π electrons of graphene and CQDs substrates enhanced the electron transfer process of C–F bonding, which provided better catalytic activity of C atoms and facilitated oxygen adsorption. Co-doping of fluorine and chlorine into graphene could further adjust the charge distribution, changed the electronic structure of the material, formed more and more stable C-halogen bonds, enhanced the charge transfer in the system, and improved performance of the catalytic electrode. Br and I atoms were less likely to be substituted doped into the carbon lattice compared to Cl atoms due to their large atomic size and were more at the edge for sites and defect sites of the electrocatalyst. Br and I might reduce the surface defects of the catalytic electrodes when doped into the defective sites, decreasing the catalytic performance of the catalytic electrode [35]. Therefore, F and Cl co-doped catalytic electrodes had the best electrocatalytic activity in double halogen atom doping based on F doping.

In the catalytic electrodes doped with single halogen atoms, ΔGH* of F-CQDs/VG was closest to zero. The electrochemical performance enhancement was best for the F-atom doped electrodes, which was consistent with the experimental part of the results (Fig. S10a). The gibbs free energy step diagrams of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG were represented in Fig. 4 a. Figs. S10b–d showed the simulation of adsorbed H atoms of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG. The FCl-CQDs/VG with ΔGH* of −0.86 eV had the best HER activity, which was consistent with the results obtained from experimental LSV data. Fig. 4b showed a schematic diagram of C–F bond modification after Cl atoms doping F-CQDs/VG. Fig. 4c indicated the schematic diagram of the effect on the electrochemical activity of the catalytic electrode after successive doping of F and Cl atoms in CQDs/VG. A small number of active sites existed in the CQDs/VG. The doping of F into CQDs/VG led to an increase in the number of active sites, and the strong electronegativity of F increased the adsorption capacity for H+ and electrons, accelerating the electrochemical process. The doping of Cl atoms into F-CQDs/VG produced favorable changes in C doped with F atoms, promoting the electronic structure and charge redistribution of C–F bonds. The covalently bonded C–F became ionic and semi-ionic bonded C–F, enhancing the adsorption of hydrogen intermediates and further reducing the Gibbs free energy. The DFT results showed that FCl-CQDs/VG had the strongest adsorption and desorption capacity for the reaction intermediates, further explaining the optimal electrochemical performance of FCl-CQDs/VG.

The magnitude of the EIS represented the charge transfer ability of the catalyst during the reaction process. As could be seen from Fig. 5 a, the dot plots were the unfitted raw EIS data, and the line plots indicated the EIS data after being fitted by the Zview software. The Rct values of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG were 7.5 Ω, 9 Ω, and 12 Ω, respectively, and FCl-CQDs/VG had the smallest resistance for charge transfer. This was attributed to the synergistic effect of F doping and Cl doping. F had the maximum electronegativity, which could adsorb the electrons of C atoms around F atoms and change the electronic states on the graphene surface, especially the presence of the semi-ionic bond C–F, which greatly facilitated the charge transfer and electron transport. Cl had a greater electron affinity compared to F, and the empty 3d orbital of Cl could accept π electrons, again promoting the charge transfer rate. The lasting stability of the catalytic electrode was also an important aspect to measure its ability to be applied. Fig. 5b indicated FCl-CQDs/VG maintained good stability in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in a long-term test of 10 h. In the previous mechanical strength test, when the VG strain reached 55 %, the strain and pressure still showed a good trend, and the material could still return to its original shape when compressed to 12.5 % of the previous volume, and the volume was basically unchanged before and after compression [49]. This was attributed to the strong mechanical strength of the oriented freeze-cast vertical graphene arrays as well as the strong mass transfer capabilities and interactions of the strong halogen bonds between the graphene sheets [50]. The electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) of the sample was calculated from the linear slope of the current density versus scan rate based on the corresponding CV data. Fig. 5c represented the Cdl values of 13.8 mF cm−2, 10.6 mF cm−2, and 7.4 mF cm−2 for FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, and FI-CQDs/VG, respectively. Fig. S11 showed the CV curves corresponding to the ECSA of FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG and FI-CQDs/VG. FCl-CQDs/VG had the largest electrochemical double-layer capacitance, indicating that it has the largest reaction area and more active sites. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was proportional to the electrochemical double-layer capacitance, and FCl-CQDs/VG still had the largest ECSA value in Fig. 5d, which was consistent with the trend obtained from LSV data. FCl-CQDs/VG had the largest Cdl value and ECSA value attributed to the 3D vertical graphene array substrate and F atoms and Cl atoms co-doping. The co-doping of F and Cl atoms not only provided a large number of active sites, and the strong electronegativity further promoted electron distribution and charge transfer. Vertical graphene arrays promoted the effective contact area between the electrolyte and the electrode, which ultimately increased the electrochemically active surface area of the catalytic electrode. Fig. 5e and f represented the prepared FCl-CQDs/VG, FBr-CQDs/VG, FI-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes as cathode and anode used as overall water splitting test in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte, respectively. The prepared catalytic electrodes were tested as cathode and anode for overall water splitting in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytes. The water splitting voltages of FCl-CQDs/VG were 1.83 V and 1.97 V, respectively, when the current densities reached 10 mA cm−2, and the water splitting current densities could reach 100 mA cm−2 when the voltages were sufficiently large to reach 3 V. This indicated that the prepared samples had the potential for the application of overall water splitting. Fig. 5g and h represented the i-t curves of the prepared FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes for doing overall water splitting tests in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions at 1.83V and 1.97V, respectively. It could be seen that the current density of the catalytic electrode hardly changed under constant voltage condition in up to 10h test, indicating the continuous stability of the catalytic electrode, which was attributed to the strong stability of the vertical graphene arrays prepared with vinasse as the carbon source.

Faraday efficiency indicated the potential of the catalyst for industrial applications. The positive and negative poles of the power supply were connected to two FCl-CQDs/VG electrodes placed in the electrolyte, and the current was adjusted up to 10 mA with a voltage of 1.88 V for Faraday efficiency testing. Fig. 6 a represented the amount of hydrogen produced versus the amount of oxygen produced by the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. When the reaction proceeded to 1200 s, the amount of hydrogen produced was 1.4 mL and the amount of oxygen produced was 0.7 mL. When the reaction proceeded to 2400 s, the amount of hydrogen produced was 2.8 mL and the amount of oxygen produced was 1.4 mL. When the reaction proceeded to 3600 s, the amount of hydrogen produced was 4 mL and the amount of oxygen produced was 2 mL. When the reaction proceeded to 4800 s, the amount of hydrogen produced was 5.6 mL and the amount of oxygen produced was 2.8 mL. When the reaction proceeded to 6000 s, the amount of hydrogen produced was 7 mL and the amount of oxygen produced was 3.5 mL. The Faraday efficiency represented the industrial feasibility of a catalytic electrode. The Faraday efficiencies of FCl-CQDs/VG at each stage of the reaction were represented in Fig. 6b. The calculated average HER Faraday efficiency of FCl-CQDs/VG was about 89 %, and the average OER Faraday efficiency was about 86.5 %, which proved that the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode had the prospect of industrial application. Fig. 6c and e represented the amount of hydrogen and the amount of oxygen produced by FBr-CQDs/VG and FI-CQDs/VG at each stage of the reaction. Fig. 6d and f represented the Faraday efficiencies of FBr-CQDs/VG and FI-CQDs/VG at each stage of the reaction in 1 M KOH. The average HER Faraday efficiencies of FBr-CQDs/VG and FI-CQDs/VG were 86.7 % and 84.8 %, and the average OER Faraday efficiencies were 84.2 % and 81.6 %, respectively. From these results, it could be concluded that FCl-CQDs/VG had the largest HER and OER Faraday efficiencies of 89 % and 86.5 %, respectively, among the same type of catalytic electrodes. This might be due to the synergistic effect of F and Cl co-doping to promote fast charge transfer and accelerate the reaction rate. The porous and lamellar structure of the vertical graphene arrays increased the contact area with the electrolyte and facilitated gas generation and release.

The full XPS spectrum in Fig. 7 a confirmed the successful doping of halogen atoms on the FClBrI-CQDs/VG electrode. The fitted peaks corresponding to CC/C–C, C–O–H, and C-halogen bonds in the C 1s high-resolution spectra at 284.8 eV, 285.58 eV, and 286.4 eV in Fig. 7b indicated that the halogen atoms were doped onto the CQDs/VG substrates. The 684.3 eV, 686.95 eV, and 689.3 eV fitted peaks in the F 1s high-resolution spectra represented ionic, semi-ionic, and covalent C–F bonds (Fig. 7c). The contents of ionic, semi-ionic and covalent bonded C–F were 24.4 %, 32.9 % and 42.7 %, respectively. The contents of ionic and semi-ionic bonds showed a significant decrease after the doping of Br and I atoms, which lowered the charge transfer rate and reduced the reactive sites, leading to the decrease of electrochemical properties. The fitted peaks at 196.6 eV, 197.2 eV, 200.28 eV and 201.78 eV in Fig. 7d represented the Cl 2p3/2, Cl 2p1/2 orbitals of ionically bonded chlorine and covalently bonded C–Cl, respectively. The content of ionically bonded chlorine showed a significant decrease compared with that of FCl-CQDs, which also reduced the synergistic interaction between F and Cl atoms and lowered the catalytic activity. The fitted peaks at 68.14 eV and 69.7 eV in Fig. 7e represented Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively, indicating that C is bonded to Br atoms [51]. The fitted peaks at 620.3 eV and 631.45 eV in Fig. 7f represented I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, which also corresponded to the presence of the polyiodide anions I3- and I5- [52]. From the XPS results, it could be seen that there are unfavorable factors when Br and I atoms are doped into FCl-CQDs/VG. On the one hand, the introduction of Br and I directly affected the binding of F atoms to CQDs/VG, reducing the content of ionic and semi-ionic C–F bonds. On the other hand, the introduction of Br and I also indirectly affected the synergistic interaction between F and Cl by reducing the content of ionic chlorine. Therefore, the doping of Br and I atoms led to a decrease in the electrochemical performance of the catalytic electrode.

Fig. S12 represented the electrochemical properties of FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, FClBrI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Figs. S12a and c indicated the LSV curves of HER of FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, FClBrI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Figs. S12b and d correspond to the tafel slopes of the HER of FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, and FClBrI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions. Similarly, Figs. S12e, f, g, and h showed the OER electrocatalytic performance of FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, and FClBrI-CQDs/VG in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions. From the results of Fig. S12, the magnitude order of electrocatalytic performance of multi-halogen atom doped graphene was FClBr-CQDs/VG > FClI-CQDs/VG > FBrI-CQDs/VG > FClBrI-CQDs/VG. But compared with the above FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes, doped multi-halogen atom graphene had poorer electrocatalytic performance. The halogen atoms Br and I were further doped in the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. The introduction of Br and I atoms caused a large spatial site resistance, leading to a decrease in the charge transfer rate. In addition, Br and I atoms also occupied the defective sites and reduced the surface defects of the catalytic electrode, which was consistent with the conclusion of the EIS and Cdl data. The doping of Br and I atoms might adversely affect the electronic structure of the catalysts, change the bonding form of C, F and Cl atoms, reduce the catalytic active sites, and diminish the promotion of the C–F bonding effect of Cl. Fig. S13a showed the magnitude of EIS values for multi-halogen atoms doped graphene as FClBr-CQDs/VG (11 Ω) < FClI-CQDs/VG (14 Ω) < FBrI-CQDs/VG (16 Ω) < FClBrI-CQDs/VG (20 Ω). From Fig. S13b, the Cdl values of FClBr-CQDs/VG, FClI-CQDs/VG, FBrI-CQDs/VG, FClBrI-CQDs/VG were 7.3 mF cm−2, 6.4 mF cm−2, 5.5 mF cm−2, and 4.5 mF cm−2, respectively. The Cdl value and EIS of the multi-halogen atom doped graphene exhibited a decrease compared to the aforementioned FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. This was attributed to the fact that Br and I were mainly doped into the edge sites and defect sites of the CQDs/VG, which reduced the surface of the catalytic electrode, and the decrease in the content of the semi-ionic bond C–F impeded the electron transport and charge transfer in the vertical graphene arrays.

As shown in Fig. 8 , we compared the electrocatalytic performance of the FCl-CQDs/VG catalytic electrodes with the electrocatalytic performance of all-carbon catalytic electrodes reported in the past three years [2,45,53–61]. The electrochemical performance of the prepared self-supported catalytic electrodes had significant advantages over the all-carbon catalysts reported in the last three years. Particularly in terms of HER performance, it was only 40–70 mV lower than the noble metal catalysts. The excellent electrochemical activity was attributed to the high mechanical strength and electron transport capacity of the vertical graphene arrays made from vinasse as the carbon source, as well as the synergistic effect among the halogen atoms.

In this paper, vinasse was prepared into CQDs and graphene by hydrothermal and extracted cellulose alkalization methods, and other halogen atoms were further introduced on the basis of F-doped CQDs/VG catalytic electrode. Among the multi-halogen atom doped forms, the best electrocatalytic performance was achieved by co-doping with F and Cl atoms. The ionic and semi-ionic bonding C–F played a major role in the electrochemical process, while the introduction of Cl atoms would further increase the ionic and semi-ionic bonding C–F content. And ionic chlorine also participated in the reaction as an electron acceptor. Further doping of Br and I atoms decreased the content of ionically bonded and semi-ionically bonded C–F, hindered the synergistic interaction between F and Cl, and decreased the electrochemical performance of the catalyst. We presented a self-supported catalytic electrode with high mechanical strength and stable 3D porous array morphology prepared from cellulose- and hemicellulose-rich biomass vinasse as a carbon source. The catalytic electrode exhibited electrochemical activity close to that of noble metals, attributed to the interaction of multi-halogen atom doping based on F doping, which contributed to a certain extent to the development of green and efficient electrolytic water hydrogen production technology.
